steps.text: Ensure calibrated electronic mass flow controllers (MFCs) are used for the diluent gas and each standard gas.
Prepare working-level standards for calibrating the GC-MS by dilution of a higher concentration stock standard gas.
Verify the gas dilution system to be non-biasing under HFC zero air and known standards.
Report the results of the calibration whenever the dilution system is used, listing the required details.
Verify the gas dilution system daily or per use as per Section 3.2 of Method 205.

constraints.text: The gas dilution system must produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
The gas dilution system must be recalibrated once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty ≤ 0.25%.
The equalization time for the bias verification must be used at a minimum for the development of standards.
Zero air must be flowed through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold used and verified to not be detectable for the target compounds.
A known standard within the calibration range must be flowed through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold for verification.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of appropriate materials (chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass).

steps.text: Include a calibrated pressure transducer or pressure gauge in the static dilution system to measure the partial pressures of each standard gas and the balance gas.
Ensure pressure transducer(s) or pressure gauge(s) used for static dilution have an accuracy of ± 0.1% full scale or 0.13 kPa, calibrated over the range of use.
Construct connection tubing for the static dilution system of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Construct manifolds for the static dilution system of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Recalibrate the static gas dilution system once per calendar year using a NIST-traceable primary pressure gauge with an uncertainty ≤ 0.1%.

Report the results of the calibration whenever the dilution system is used, listing the date of the most recent calibration, the due date for the next calibration, calibration point, reference flow device (ID, S:N ratio), and acceptance criteria.
Verify the gas dilution system to be non-biasing under HFC zero air and known standards at least once per calendar year for each reactive target compound.
Flow zero air through all applicable tubing and manifold and verify it is not detectable for the target compounds.

Flow a known standard within the calibration range of the analytical system for each target compound through all applicable tubing and manifold into the standard canister or vessel and verify it does not bias the standard by ± 15% of the concentrations in the reference sample.
Ensure the calibrated hygrometer can achieve a 1% RH resolution with a yearly calibration to a NIST-traceable accuracy of ± 3% RH within the range of 20% to 80% RH.

Verify the calibration of the hydrometer weekly or per use (whichever is less stringent) at a single point that is approximately 40 to 50% RH to within ± 5% using a second calibrated hygrometer or a saturated salt solution.

Use only NIST-certified or NIST-traceable calibration standards, standard reference materials, and reagents that are stable through certification and recertification for the tests and procedures required by this method.
Use standards and reagents within their expiration period and evaluate working-level standards prepared in canisters within 30 days of preparation.
Ensure the concentrations of the target compounds in the mixture are commensurate with the anticipated dilution factor needed to dilute the mixture to the desired working range.
Retain and report the gas standard certificates of analysis.
Flush the sampling device or system with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for a minimum of 24 hours before field deployment.
Verify that the analytical equipment used in performing this method meets the performance criteria.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation is non-biasing under zero-air.
Analyze humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air from a known clean source at the installation prior to initial use of the instrument.
Examine chromatograms for interferences and other chromatographic artifacts.
Take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Repeat the analytical zero-air verification to ensure that you have mitigated any problems before using the analytical instrumentation.

Close the valve, remove the vacuum/pressure gauge, and loosely cap the canister using a cap fitting to ensure that leakage through the valve is accurately assessed while avoiding potential entry of debris into the valve during storage.
After a minimum of three days in storage, reinstall the vacuum/pressure gauge, open the valve, and record the canister pressure reading.
Determine the pressure decay rate as the absolute value of the difference between the initial and post-storage canister pressures.
You must remove the canister from service if the pressure decay rate exceeds 0.69 kPa/storage day (0.2 in. Hg or 0.1 psia/storage day).
You must qualify each canister as being acceptably non-biasing under zero-air conditions to ensure sample validity.
Qualify new canisters before initial use and qualify all canisters used for sampling at least once every 6 months.
Pressurize the clean evacuated canister with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air.
Do not use ultrapure nitrogen to pressurize the canister because the inert nitrogen atmosphere does not permit reactions within the canister that may occur under sampling conditions.
Allow the canister to equilibrate for a minimum of 24 hours.
After the equilibration period, conduct an initial cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.4 of this method.
Store the canister for a holding period equal to or exceeding the typical laboratory holding time, nominally 7 days from the canister fill date.
After the holding period, conduct a subsequent cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.5 of this method.
The results of both the initial and subsequent cleanliness analysis must meet the cleanliness criteria specified in Section 8.5 of this method to be used for sampling.
You must reclean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air challenge.
If necessary, use more aggressive cleaning techniques such as water rinses or other rinses as specified by manufacturers.
If a canister continues to fail the zero-air challenges, remove the canister from service.
You must qualify each canister as being acceptably non-biasing under known-standard conditions to ensure sample validity.
Qualify new canisters before initial use and qualify all canisters used for sampling at least every 6 months.
Fill the clean evacuated canister with a humidified (40 to 50% RH) standard gas in HCF zero air with each target compound at approximately 100 to 500 pptv.
Choose the selected challenge concentration based on the concentration expected to be measured during the sampling event.
Prepare and secure the canisters for transport.
Prepare at least seven blank samples according to the procedures in Section 9.3.2 using sampling media that have been deployed in the field, and cleaned per Section 8.4.
Prepare at least seven spike samples according to the procedures in either Section 10.2 or 10.3 at a concentration of the target compound within a factor of five of the expected detection limits.
Compute the standard deviation for the replicate blank samples concentrations and multiply this value by 3.14 to determine the blank MDL (MDLb).
Compute the standard deviation for the replicate spike sample concentrations and multiply this value by 3.14 to determine the spike MDL (MDLs).
Select the greater of MDLb or MDLs as the MDL for the compound of interest.
Repeat MDL determinations at least annually or whenever significant changes have been made to the sampling or analytical system.

preconditions.text: If the MDL does not meet the concentration requirement, perform corrective action and repeat the MDL determination.

steps.text: Take steps to remove contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Test to demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation (preconcentrator and GC-MS system) is not causing loss of compounds (negative bias).
Check for cold spots in transfer line, gas impurities, sorbent traps, GC column, system leaks, and/or poor moisture management.
After establishing the ICAL, test each port of the autosampler to demonstrate cleanliness (positive bias) by analyzing humidified zero air.
Heat and purge any lines, and/or rinse with deionized water, dry, and purge any lines that fail.

preconditions.text: Before testing the analytical instrumentation, analysis must show that any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample are at response levels that are expected to be < 20 pptv or not detected.
Prior to initial use of the autosampler or after analysis of potentially contaminating samples.

steps.text: Clean and retest canisters that fail the known-standard verification.
Remove canisters from service that cannot pass known-standard verification after the cleaning process.
Take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the sampling unit (e.g., purging with HCF zero air overnight or longer).
Disassemble and clean.

preconditions.text: Prior to initial field deployment and every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples.

Prior to initial field deployment and at least every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples or damaging sample matrices that may impact the activity of the flow path surfaces.

steps.text: Analyze a minimum of five calibration levels covering approximately 20 to 5000 pptv.
Flag data for possible invalidation.
Immediately after each ICAL, analyze a secondary source standard in the lower third of the calibration curve to verify ICAL accuracy for each target analyte.
Flag data for possible invalidation.
Prior to analyzing samples in an analytical sequence, analyze a known standard in the lower third of the calibration curve to verify ongoing instrument calibration for each target analyte.

preconditions.text: Before sample analysis, following failed BFB tune check, failed IS criteria, or failed CCV criteria; annually, or when changes/maintenance to the instrument affect calibration response.
Prior to analyzing samples in an analytical sequence and at the end of a sequence, unless the sequence begins with an ICAL; recommended after every 10 sample injections.

steps.text: Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion unless there is a spectral interference.
Invalidate then reanalyze any samples for which the IS area response differs by more than 30% from the mean IS area response.
Calibrate the GC-MS initially, annually, or when CCV standards exceed acceptance criteria.
Prior to calibration, analyze a sufficient number of humidified HCF zero air blanks or humidified check standards to verify that instrument sensitivity is stable.
Prepare the calibration curve by preparing standards that bracket the expected concentration levels at the sampling location(s).
Include at least five levels in the ICAL to approximate concentrations of target oHAPs.
Prepare a calibration curve for each target analyte by determining the relative response factor (RRF) of each concentration level.
Calculate the RRF of each target compound in each calibration level using Equation 11.
Choose the quantitation ion for each target compound as the most abundant ion unless there is a spectral interference.
Ensure the %RSD of the RRF for each target compound must be ≤ 30%.
Ensure the calculated concentration for each target compound at each calibration level is within ± 30% of the theoretical concentration.
Review the sample collection information documented on the field data page and/or COC form(s) for completeness and accuracy.
Compare the canister label with the sample collection data sheet and verify that the canister and sample IDs are correct.
Measure and record the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or transducer.
Flag the results as invalid if the measured canister absolute pressure is not within ±3.5 kPa of that measured upon collection in the field.
Pressurize the canister with diluent gas to a pressure less than or equal to the final pressure of the standard gas canisters.
Measure the canister pressure just prior to dilution and immediately following dilution.
Calculate the canister dilution correction factor (CDCF) from the two absolute pressure readings.
Allow diluted canisters to equilibrate for a minimum of 12 hours before analysis.
Draw a measured aliquot of the whole air sample from the sample canister by vacuum through a preconcentrator.

constraints.text: The measured canister absolute pressure must be within ±3.5 kPa of that measured upon collection in the field.
You must allow diluted canisters to equilibrate for a minimum of 12 hours before analysis.

steps.text: Perform an air/water check of the MS prior to any analyses to ensure that the system is acceptably leak-free.
Conduct a thorough system bakeout per the manufacturer’s instructions for the preconcentrator and ramp the GC column temperature.
Analyze a preliminary IB or perform the BFB instrument tuning check.
Analyze a laboratory MB to demonstrate that the system is acceptably clean and that each target compound is < 20 pptv or undetected (whichever is more stringent) per compound of interest.
Analyze a CCV to verify the instrument calibration.
Analyze field samples and additional CCV standards (every 10 samples) and MBs to complete the sequence, ending with a CCV, as discussed in Section 9.2 of this method.
After completing data acquisition, examine each chromatogram.
Identify target compounds qualitatively based on their RT and the relative abundance of their characteristic ions from the MS by satisfying the following four criteria.

preconditions.text: If the on-column concentration of any compound in any sample exceeds the calibration range, you must dilute the sample for reanalysis by either reducing the sample aliquot volume for an effective dilution or adding diluent gas to the sample canister to physically dilute the sample.
